answer:
**Iqbal's *Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam* (Bang-e-Dara Part 3, Ch. 13) invokes the memory of early Muslim forebears—desert Arabs who conquered empires through proud action—to shame modern youth into revival.**[^1] The most demanding line indicts their inertia: "You can have no true kinship with your forefathers, for you are all talk, they were all action; you are static, they were ever-moving."[^2]

**Manu’s Chapter 2 details the *brahmachārin*'s (celibate student's) rigorous formation through ritual purity, sensory restraint, alms-begging, and total subservience to the *guru*, preparing him for *varṇa*-duty.**[^3] The most demanding line enforces absolute celibacy: "He should sleep alone everywhere and should never spill his seed; for by spilling his seed from desire, he breaks his vow."[^4]

**Iqbal's motivational pedagogy—rousing youth via ancestral heroism—presupposes a dynamic, expansionist polity of collective Muslim action and world-seizing (recalling the caliphal/imperial *ummah* forged from tribal poverty).** Manu's disciplinarian regimen—ego-crushing service and ritual—presupposes the static, hierarchical Vedic polity of fixed *varṇas*, priestly authority, and dharma-sustaining kingship.[^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 13 (*Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam*), [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-365462](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 2, [translation](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/).
[^4]: Ibid., [p-51cabf](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-51cabf).
[^5]: *Manusmṛti* 2.6–12, 136–57 (sources of dharma, grounds of respect); cf. Iqbal's portrait of action-oriented forebears, [*p-365462*](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462).

args.query: brahmacharin OR student OR "celibate student" manu
